summary_text:
S:
The patient reports a risk of a strong reaction to blood products and frequent infections. He also reports pain in the left hand and severe diarrhea. The patient believes their cold symptoms are unusual.

O:
Blood pressure is mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. The patient has a history of iga deficiency.

A:
The patient has type two diabetes and an iga deficiency. He may experience respiratory tract infections, sinus infections, and abdominal infections. The patient's family owns a donut shop.

P:
Start metformin. Order blood transfusions. Follow up in one week to assess response to treatment. Schedule a return visit if symptoms worsen.
reference_summary:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Kyle Lee is a pleasant 46-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for evaluation of low immunoglobulin A level. He was referred to our offices by his primary care physician after routine labs revealed abnormalities. The patient states he has been experiencing frequent infections. During the winter months, he experiences frequent colds that tend to linger, however this is not uncommon for him. The patient denies abdominal issues or diarrhea.

The patient was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. He is currently taking metformin.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient denies a history of blood transfusions.

SOCIAL HISTORY

He recently traveled internationally on a trip to Zambia. His family owns a local doughnut shop.

FAMILY HISTORY

Patient denies a family history of immune deficiencies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Gastrointestinal: Denies abdominal issues or diarrhea.

VITALS

Temperature: Normal.
All other vitals were reviewed and are within normal limits.

PHYSICAL EXAM

NECK: No swelling noted. No lymphadenopathy.
CV: Normal heart rhythm with no murmurs.
RESPIRATORY: Lungs are clear. There's no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
Gastrointestinal: Abdomen is soft and without tenderness.

RESULTS

I did review the results of her recent lab work. It is consistent as her primary care physician noted with an IgA deficiency.

ASSESSMENT

IgA deficiency.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that the results of his recent lab work is consistent with an IgA deficiency. I have recommended that we obtain additional blood work to check his other antibodies. I encouraged the patient to be aware of lingering infections or abdominal changes including diarrhea. If he experiences these issues, he should report them to my office so we may investigate further, however I do not believe there is a need for that at this time. In the meantime, he should report this IgA deficiency in the event that he needs any blood transfusions. Questions were invited and answered today.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient has a history of iga deficiency.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Past medical history belongs in the subjective section unless it is being reported as a new objective finding; here it is simply stated as history, not as a new test result.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient has type two diabetes and an iga deficiency.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are established diagnoses/past medical history and are not being newly synthesized or interpreted; they function as background history and fit better in the subjective section.

section_miscategorizations.text: He may experience respiratory tract infections, sinus infections, and abdominal infections.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is anticipatory counseling about expected/recurrent infections, which is closer to patient education/management planning than diagnostic synthesis, though its placement in Assessment is not highly misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's family owns a donut shop.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is social history, not diagnostic assessment or problem synthesis, and should not appear in the Assessment section.

A plan to order blood transfusions without any corresponding anemia/bleeding diagnosis or rationale in the Assessment creates a disconnect; the need for transfusion should be grounded in an Assessment statement describing the underlying problem.
